summary: The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held a special meeting on Saturday, February 26, 2022, to approve a corrected pipeline easement resolution for Duke Energy and to conduct a work session on economic development. Mike Hennecamp of Ohio Plan presented an educational overview of economic development tools available to Ohio townships, including enterprise zones, CRAs, TIFs, RIDs, JEDs, and new community authorities. The presentation emphasized Goshen's predominantly residential zoning as a long-term financial sustainability concern and encouraged the board to consider diversifying revenue sources as the township grows. No decisions on economic development strategies were made; the session was informational only.
decisions: Approved Resolution R-011-2022 authorizing the administrator to execute a pipeline easement to Duke Energy of Ohio Inc., replacing Resolution R-010-2022 which had typographical errors
action_items: Board to consider expediting the update of the township's land use plan as recommended by Mike Hennecamp
Administrator Pecrum to facilitate future conversations about which economic development tools may be appropriate for Goshen
Consider forming a CIC (Community Improvement Corporation) as identified in the strategic plan
Consider consulting with Andy Brossard (economic development finance expert) if the board decides to pursue any specific tools
Prepare economic development strategies as outlined in the township's strategic plan
key_amounts: One mill of taxation generates approximately $300,000 in Goshen Township
Springfield Township's JED/JEDZ generates $3.5-$4 million per year
Springfield Township's TIF district generates approximately $1 million per year
Enterprise zones can abate up to 60% of assessed property value for up to 15 years (can exceed with school board approval)
TIF exemption can be up to 75% of assessed value, up to 30 years with school board approval
Mount Healthy receives approximately $600,000/year from its JEDZ partnership with Springfield Township
notable_topics: Approval of corrected Duke Energy pipeline easement resolution (R-011-2022)
Review of Goshen Township strategic plan and SWOT analysis from 2020-2021
Concern that predominantly residential zoning (~80%) threatens long-term financial sustainability
Enterprise zones as economic development tool (Bryant Equipment cited as local example)
Community Reinvestment Areas (CRAs) for residential and retail incentives
Tax Increment Financing (TIFs) as revenue diversion tool for infrastructure
Residential Improvement Districts (RIDs) as residential equivalent of TIFs
Joint Economic Development Districts (JEDs) as new income tax revenue source for townships
New Community Authorities (NCAs) as development financing tool
Springfield Township comparisons and lessons learned from bedroom community challenges
Importance of updating the township land use plan
attendees: Bob (attendee familiar with TIFs who asked questions about statutory townships)
notes: The transcript contains some confusion in roll call names (Trustee Allen, Trustee Hodges appear inconsistently with Trustee Young and Trustee Hoffman). The meeting was explicitly stated to be informational with no votes or decisions expected on economic development matters. Mike Hennecamp drew heavily on his experience managing Springfield Township (Hamilton County) to illustrate pros and cons of various tools. The presentation referenced the Amelia dissolution as a cautionary tale about TIF obligations. Andy Brossard was recommended as a future consultant but was unavailable to attend. The transcript appears truncated before the full discussion concluded.
